writing_points.source_text: Zinc-Aluminum-Magnesium (Zn-Al-Mg) coated steel is an emerging environmentally friendly material with excellent corrosion resistance and self-repairing corrosion protection property. However, the presence of the Zn- Al-Mg coating poses significant challenges during welding, including severe coating evaporation, complex metallurgical reactions, coating cracking in the heat-affected zone (HAZ), elemental segregation, and micro­ structural inhomogeneity. In this study, a novel spatio-temporal beam shaping strategy-combining full domain power modulation with galvanometer-scanning laser was proposed to achieve precise control of the molten pool through synergistic energy and mechanical coupling. High-quality laser lap joints of Zn-Al-Mg coated steel were successfully obtained. The effects of laser power modulation amplitude on molten pool dynamics, spatter behavior, weld formation, and microstructure were systematically investigated. The results demonstrate that the tailored beam shaping strategy enhances molten pool stability, reduces spatter, and improves weld morphology. Quantitative analysis reveals that at a 40% power modulation amplitude, the maximum weld surface depression depth is reduced by 22.6%, and the HAZ width decreases by 23.9% compared to the unmodulated process, effectively mitigating coating cracking. Furthermore, the average grain size in the weld zone is refined by 22.5% (decreasing from 46.6 μm to 36.1 μm), while the proportion of high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) increases by 13.37%. This leads to a lower temperature gradient in the molten pool, a wider equiaxed grain zone, and refined grain structures. Moreover, the formation mechanism of equiaxed grains and grain refinement behavior under different modulation amplitudes were analyzed based on classical nucleation and growth theory. This work provides an effective approach for stabilizing the laser welding process and optimizing the microstructure and mechanical performance of Zn-Al-Mg coated steels.

writing_points.source_text: Zn-Al-Mg coated steel is a novel environmentally friendly coating material. Due to its excellent corrosion resistance, self-healing proper­ ties, and superior anti-rust performance, it has gradually replaced some stainless steels and aluminum alloys in various engineering applications, including photovoltaic brackets, rail transportation, and construction sectors [1,2]. The Zn-Al-Mg coating possesses a unique self-repairing corrosion protection property against cut-edge corrosion, attributed to the formation of a passivation film. This feature provides superior pro­ tection for both incisions and surface scratches. Unlike traditional galvanized coatings, when Zn-Al-Mg steel is sheared or scratched, a dense passivation film (composed of Zn-Al-Mg oxides and carbonates) forms on the exposed substrate. writing_points.source_text: The welding of Zn-Al-Mg coated steel faces three major challenges. First, low-boiling-point elements such as zinc (Zn) and magnesium (Mg) evaporate intensely during laser welding, generating high-pressure metal vapor that violently disrupts the molten pool and keyhole stabil­ ity. This leads to welding defects such as spatter, undercutting, and porosity, severely compromising joint formation quality [5,6]. Furthermore, Al tends to segregate near the weld surface and fusion line, promoting δ-ferrite coarsening and the formation of coarse primary ferrite grains. This results in elemental segregation, microstructural heterogeneity, and compositional inhomogeneity in the weld, signifi­ cantly reducing joint mechanical properties [9,10]. In addition, due to the high hardness of the Zn-Al-Mg coating, the heat-affected zones (HAZ) on both sides of the weld are prone to surface coating cracking under thermal stresses, which reduces the corrosion resistance of the welded area. These welding issues make the stable welding of Zn-Al-Mg coated steel a persistent challenge and severely hinder its broader application and industrial promotion.

writing_points.source_text: In response to the complex requirements of multi-process optimiza­ tion in the laser lap welding of Zn-Al-Mg coated steel, this study pro­ poses a novel spatio-temporal beam shaping strategy based on “full domain power modulation combined with galvanometer-scanning laser”, which couples beam oscillation in the spatial dimension with power modulation in the temporal dimension. A laser welding system was developed that integrates the advantages of both temporal and spatial beam shaping, enabling precise control of the molten pool through thermo-mechanical coupling. By implementing a modulation pattern with higher central and lower peripheral power, the energy is redistributed within the molten pool, allowing stress regulation in the HAZ without sacrificing thermal efficiency or weld formation quality. This enables coordinated control of coating evaporation, molten pool metallurgy, and elemental segregation. Preliminary experimental results have demonstrated that the system allows for fine-tuning of the laser energy distribution to match the energy field requirements, significantly expanding the ability to control molten pool behavior and weld morphology. It effectively suppresses welding defects such as spatter, porosity, and cracking, thereby improving joint quality [26–28].

writing_points.claim: Oscillating laser beams also introduce a stirring effect on the molten pool, which helps to homogenize the distribution of aluminum and improve the hardness uniformity of the weld [14].
writing_points.source_text: Mechanical grinding to remove the surface coating is a commonly used pretreatment method for welding coated steels, as it effectively avoids the negative effects caused by the coating [11]. However, this approach introduces an additional processing step and increases manufacturing costs. Beam oscillation techniques, such as circular or eight patterns, have been shown to homogenize laser energy distribu­ tion, reduce laser energy density and temperature gradients in the molten pool, thereby enhancing the stability of both the molten pool and keyhole, and reducing spatter formation [12,13]. Oscillating laser beams also introduce a stirring effect on the molten pool, which helps to homogenize the distribution of aluminum and improve the hardness uniformity of the weld [14]. In addition, methods such as pulsed laser, power-modulated laser, and optimized process parameters can also improve the distribution and content of aluminum, mitigating the adverse effects of coating elements on the weld without incurring the high costs of traditional mechanical grinding [15]. However, these techniques still face limitations in comprehensively addressing the multiple challenges encountered in the laser welding of Zn-Al-Mg coated steels, including severe coating evaporation, complex metallurgical re­ actions, high residual stresses in the heat-affected zone (HAZ), elemental segregation, and microstructural inhomogeneity.

writing_points.claim: this continuous stir­ ring effectively fragments dendrites and suppresses aluminum segrega­ tion, which is crucial for the elemental homogenization of the Zn-Al-Mg coated steel molten pool.
writing_points.source_text: In the preliminary stage of this study, four different oscillation tra­ jectories (linear, 8-shaped, infinity, and circular) were compared. Experimental results indicated that, unlike asymmetric trajectories, the circular oscillation trajectory possesses a unique central symmetry. This geometric characteristic ensures a more isotropic and uniform distri­ bution of laser energy at the edges of the molten pool, thereby pre­ venting localized overheating or lack of fusion. Furthermore, circular oscillation induces a stable rotational vortex flow; this continuous stir­ ring effectively fragments dendrites and suppresses aluminum segrega­ tion, which is crucial for the elemental homogenization of the Zn-Al-Mg coated steel molten pool.

writing_points.claim: In terms of both mathematical modeling and practical control, it facilitates the synthesis of a controllable uniform energy field resembling a Gaussian distribu­ tion, a feat difficult to achieve precisely with complex trajectories like the 8-shaped pattern.
writing_points.source_text: Additionally, the circular trajectory synergizes perfectly with gradient power modulation (high center, low edge). In terms of both mathematical modeling and practical control, it facilitates the synthesis of a controllable uniform energy field resembling a Gaussian distribu­ tion, a feat difficult to achieve precisely with complex trajectories like the 8-shaped pattern. However, under the conventional circular oscil­ lation mode, a significant energy gradient persists between the peaks at the periphery and the center of the molten pool, exhibiting a charac­ teristic 'dual-peak' energy distribution. For Zn-Al-Mg coated steel, this edge-concentrated energy induces excessive thermal stress within the heat-affected zone (HAZ), which directly leads to coating cracking and elemental burnout. Therefore, in this study, the circular oscillation pattern was selected as the baseline trajectory for the beam shaping strategy. A gradient power modulation mode (characterized by high power at the center and low power at the edges) was implemented to reduce the laser power at the lateral sides, thereby homogenizing the laser energy distribution. This strategy not only preserves the coating in the HAZ (solving the cracking issue) but also decreases the overall temperature gradient (improving weld formation), serving as a tailored solution specifically designed to address the metallurgical challenges of Zn-Al-Mg alloys.

writing_points.claim: In this work, the effects of the proposed beam shaping strategy on laser energy distribution, weld formation quality, and microstructural evolution in Zn-Al-Mg coated steel lap welding were investigated.
writing_points.source_text: In this work, the effects of the proposed beam shaping strategy on laser energy distribution, weld formation quality, and microstructural evolution in Zn-Al-Mg coated steel lap welding were investigated. High- speed imaging was employed to capture spatter characteristics and molten pool dynamics, while electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) was used to analyze weld microstructures. The mechanisms by which this strategy mitigates HAZ coating cracking and refines microstructures were also elucidated. This study provides both technical guidance and theoretical support for the control of welding process stability and the regulation of microstructure and properties in Zn-Al-Mg coated steels.

writing_points.claim: Adjusting the laser beam's input power waveform or spatial profile to achieve a desired intensity distribution allows for multidimensional improvement of joint perfor­ mance [20,21].
writing_points.source_text: Laser beam shaping has become one of the commonly used tech­ niques for regulating energy distribution in the laser welding of coated steels. It can effectively reduce welding defects, improve weld forma­ tion, and enhance weld performance [16]. Traditional single-laser welding generally exhibits a Gaussian energy distribution, where the energy peak at the center of the beam is significantly higher than at the edges. This results in steep temperature gradients and unstable molten pool behavior, making the process prone to porosity and cracking [17–19]. By modulating the laser beam in temporal or spatial di­ mensions, process stability can be significantly improved, defects sup­ pressed, and weld morphology enhanced. Adjusting the laser beam's input power waveform or spatial profile to achieve a desired intensity distribution allows for multidimensional improvement of joint perfor­ mance [20,21]. Compared with Gaussian beams, shaped beams with uniform energy distribution significantly reduce internal thermal gra­ dients, thereby enhancing molten pool stability. Moreover, beam oscil­ lation facilitates the escape of trapped gas bubbles, reducing porosity, and refines grains to promote uniform phase distribution within the fusion zone, ultimately improving weld microstructure and joint quality [22,23]. Compared with constant-power laser welding, sinusoidally modulated laser welding has also been shown to enhance keyhole sta­ bility, improve weld formation, and increase tensile strength and elon­ gation of the joint [24,25]. In summary, laser beam shaping effectively reduces welding defects, improves weld quality, and enhances joint performance. However, existing laser heat source designs are often limited to one-dimensional (temporal or spatial) modulation and lack flexibility in controlling the complex physical and metallurgical pro­ cesses involved in Zn-Al-Mg coated steel welding. They are insufficient to holistically regulate the evaporation of Zn, Al, and Mg, their distri­ bution, and interfacial reactions within the molten pool, nor can they effectively resolve elemental segregation and microstructural coars­ ening. writing_points.source_text: In this study, Zn-Al-Mg coated steel sheets with dimensions of 150 mm × 50 mm × 1.5 mm were used as the experimental material. The coating thickness was approximately 30–35 μm, and the composition of the coating was Zn-11%Al-3%Mg-0.2%Si-Re. The substrate was a low- carbon steel designated as SCS51D, with its chemical composition lis­ ted in Table 1. High-purity argon gas (99.999%) was used as the shielding gas during welding, with a flow rate of 15 L/min. Laser lap welding was performed using a galvanometer-based laser welding sys­ tem with full domain power modulation developed by the current research group at Lanzhou University of Technology. A schematic dia­ gram of the welding process is shown in Fig. 1. The galvanometer laser head was tilted at an angle of 10◦in the welding direction to prevent laser reflection from damaging internal optical components. A butt joint configuration without beveling was adopted, and the assembly gap was controlled to be less than 0.2 mm. Before welding, the sample surfaces were cleaned with acetone to remove contaminants such as dirt and grease.

writing_points.source_text: The spatio-temporal beam shaping strategy employed in this study coupled beam oscillation in the spatial dimension with power modula­ tion in the temporal dimension. A galvanometer scanning system was utilized to execute high-frequency circular scanning, where the laser output power was synchronized with the phase position of the beam along the circular trajectory. We specifically used a “Gradient Power Mode”, where higher power was applied at the weld center (front and rear of the trajectory) and lower power was applied at the weld edges (sides of the trajectory). As illustrated in Fig. 2(a), the size of the circles represents the magnitude of the laser power. High power was applied when the beam is at the front and rear of the trajectory (weld center line) to ensure penetration, while low power was applied at the sides (weld edges) to reduce thermal stress on the HAZ. The modulation intensity was defined by the amplitude parameter (Tn), where T4 represented a 40% power reduction at the edges compared to the center.

writing_points.claim: Three welding experiments were conducted using three different laser power modulation amplitudes of 0%, 20%, and 40%, correspond­ ing respectively to conventional galvanometer laser (no modulation), 20% power modulation, and 40% power modulation.
writing_points.source_text: Three welding experiments were conducted using three different laser power modulation amplitudes of 0%, 20%, and 40%, correspond­ ing respectively to conventional galvanometer laser (no modulation), 20% power modulation, and 40% power modulation. These conditions were labeled as T0, T2, and T4. The scanning trajectories and power modulation waveforms are shown in Fig. 2. Within one circular scanning cycle of the galvanometer laser, the laser power at 36 phase points was controlled. The laser power at each phase point was calculated as the product of the set power coefficient at that phase, a power factor of 60%, and the total laser output power of 6 kW. The power factor defines the baseline output power as a percentage of the maximum laser power (6 kW). A power factor of 60% was selected, corresponding to an effective power of 3.6 kW, which was optimized through preliminary experiments to ensure full penetration while preventing burn-through and excessive coating evaporation. Specifically, a maximum power cap of 3.6 kW was applied in the modulation mode. The power configuration exhibits symmetry for every quarter scanning cycle (90◦). The modulated power values for the 10 phase points (starting from 0◦) within the initial quarter range (0◦-90◦) are detailed in Table 2, and the power values for the remaining points follow the symmetric waveform patterns shown in Fig. 2(b), (c), and (d). The welding speed was set to 30 mm/s, the scanning trajectory was circular with a radius of 0.5 mm, and the scanning frequency was 150 Hz.

writing_points.claim: With increasing laser power modulation amplitude, the weld surface became smoother and flatter, the amount of molten and eroded coating residues on both sides of the weld gradually decreased, and the cracks in the base metal surface were progressively reduced.
writing_points.source_text: The surface morphologies of welds under different laser power modulation amplitudes are shown in Fig. 6. The weld surface exhibited a bright metallic appearance with straight edges, accompanied by a small amount of spatter distributed on both sides of the weld. The coating near the weld surface was melted and eroded, leaving yellow–white mixed residues on both sides of the weld. In addition, coating cracks were observed in the base metal regions adjacent to both the top and bottom sides of the weld, forming irregular crack-like patterns. A small number of spatter particles were also present on the backside of the weld. With increasing laser power modulation amplitude, the weld surface became smoother and flatter, the amount of molten and eroded coating residues on both sides of the weld gradually decreased, and the cracks in the base metal surface were progressively reduced. These results indicate that increasing the laser power modulation amplitude can effectively miti­ gate coating ablation and cracking along both sides of the weld.

writing_points.source_text: To analyze the effect of laser power modulation amplitude on weld surface formation quality, the three-dimensional (3D) morphology of weld surfaces under different modulation amplitudes was characterized using a 3D measuring microscope. The results are shown in Fig. 8. Fig. 8 (a-1), (b-1), and (c-1) present the 3D height distribution maps of the weld surface, in which the color variation indicates surface height dif­ ferences. Darker colors (from red to blue, representing high to low) correspond to greater depression depths. Under all three modes, weld surfaces exhibited varying degrees of depression, with the color distri­ bution indicating a gradual decrease in surface height from the edges toward the center. As the modulation amplitude increased, the surface depression depth decreased. Specifically, in the T0 mode, the weld surface exhibited the most significant depression, with a maximum depth of 434 μm. In contrast, the maximum depression depths under T2 and T4 modes were reduced to 368 μm and 336 μm, representing de­ creases of 15.2% and 22.6%, respectively, compared with T0. These results indicate that a higher modulation amplitude enhances molten pool stability, thereby effectively improving the surface depression of the weld. In addition, both transverse and longitudinal surface profiles and roughness of the weld region were evaluated. Fig. 8(a-2), (b-2), and (c-2) show the transverse profile curves of the weld surface under different modulation amplitudes. In the T0 mode, the weld surface exhibited severe depression and poor symmetry in the profile curve. With increasing modulation amplitude, the weld surface profile became progressively smoother and more symmetric. In particular, under the T4 mode, the average transverse height difference decreased from 402.6 μm in the T0 mode to 286.6 μm, corresponding to a 28.8% reduction. Fig. 8 (a-3), (b-3), and (c-3) display the longitudinal roughness curves of the weld surface. In the T0 mode, the roughness curve showed large fluc­ tuations, pronounced undulations, and overall higher roughness, indi­ cating poor surface quality. With increasing modulation amplitude, the surface roughness improved as the fluctuation amplitude gradually decreased, although some high-frequency fluctuations were still present, indicating moderate surface flatness. When the modulation amplitude increased to 40%, the longitudinal roughness curve exhibited the smallest fluctuation amplitude and a smoother overall profile, suggest­ ing the best weld surface quality. In summary, increasing the laser power modulation amplitude reduces weld surface depression, decreases sur­ face roughness, and effectively improves the overall surface formation quality of the weld.

The average weld hardness values under T0, T2, and T4 modes are 171.3 HV, 173.4 HV, and 174.3 HV, respec­ tively—all higher than that of the base metal. The hardness across the welded joint increases from the base metal region toward the weld center, forming a “π”-shaped distribution. During welding, the Zn-Al-Mg coating melts and flows into the molten pool, allowing Al from the coating to enter the weld metal. The presence of Al can influence the weld microstructure and mechanical properties. As a ferrite-forming element, Al generally remains in solid solution in the matrix when its concentration is below 4%, contributing to solid solution strengthening, grain refinement, and improved strength and hardness. However, when Al segregates locally and its concentration increases, Al-rich ferrite may form in the fusion zone, leading to localized increases in hardness. Yoon et al. [37] reported that Al segregation in the weld metal caused by the dissolution of Al from the coating can affect phase transformation and ferrite formation at the fusion boundary and within the fusion zone. In addition, Wang et al. [14] found that beam oscillation can enhance the stirring of the molten pool, promoting a more uniform distribution of Al in the weld and improving hardness uniformity. Therefore, as the laser power modulation amplitude increases, the distribution of Al within the weld becomes more uniform, contributing to the gradual increase in average weld hardness.

This reduces the longitudinal temperature gradient and further alters the energy distri­ bution within the molten pool, thereby optimizing the solidification conditions of the liquid metal. According to the results of Chen et al. [40], a circularly oscillating laser beam can generate a more stable flow field, which helps reduce the temperature gradient and increase the solidification rate. The rotating laser beam stirs the molten pool, form­ ing a stable vortex flow that accelerates the molten metal movement and periodically drives surrounding liquid metal toward the rear of the pool. Additionally, the rotating keyhole causes periodic temperature fluctu­ ations at the solid–liquid interface near the pool boundary, leading to the stagnation, fragmentation, or remelting of columnar dendrites and suppressing their growth. Meanwhile, the rotating vortex flow drives fragmented dendrite pieces into the molten pool interior, providing additional nucleation sites for the formation of equiaxed grains and thus expanding the equiaxed zone.

writing_points.source_text: By integrating the classical theories discussed above, the influence of power modulation on the weld microstructure can be interpreted from the perspective of thermodynamic and kinetic pathways. Increasing the laser power modulation amplitude modifies the spatial energy distri­ bution within the molten pool, leading to a reduction in the temperature gradient (G). Simultaneously, due to the diminished molten pool di­ mensions and the accelerated cooling rate, the equivalent solidification rate (R) is enhanced. Consequently, the G/R ratio decreases, while the cooling rate (G⋅R) increases.

writing_points.source_text: In conventional galvanometer laser welding, when the rotating laser beam moves to the front of the molten pool, most of the energy is consumed in melting the solid metal, leaving insufficient energy to sustain the keyhole, resulting in keyhole instability. In contrast, the modulated laser employs a gradient power mode with higher power in the center and lower power at the edges. This ensures that the power at the front of the molten pool is greater than that at the sides, compen­ sating for the energy loss due to melting of the solid base metal and improving keyhole stability, thereby suppressing process-induced porosity caused by keyhole instability.

The microstructural evolution during weld solidification is intimately associated with both G and R. A reduction in G/R promotes the columnar-to-equiaxed transition (CET), whereas an increase in G⋅R contributes to grain refinement. Therefore, by adjusting the laser power modulation amplitude, a synergistic regu­ lation of G and R can be achieved qualitatively (without the need for explicit solutions to Eqs. (10)–(13). This mechanism effectively pro­ motes the formation of fine equiaxed grains. This theoretical analysis is consistent with the EBSD results observed in this study, specifically the increased fraction of equiaxed grains, reduced average grain size, and higher proportion of high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs).

selected_writing_points.source_text: Zinc-Aluminum-Magnesium (Zn-Al-Mg) coated steel is an emerging environmentally friendly material with excellent corrosion resistance and self-repairing corrosion protection property. However, the presence of the Zn- Al-Mg coating poses significant challenges during welding, including severe coating evaporation, complex metallurgical reactions, coating cracking in the heat-affected zone (HAZ), elemental segregation, and micro­ structural inhomogeneity. In this study, a novel spatio-temporal beam shaping strategy-combining full domain power modulation with galvanometer-scanning laser was proposed to achieve precise control of the molten pool through synergistic energy and mechanical coupling. High-quality laser lap joints of Zn-Al-Mg coated steel were successfully obtained. The effects of laser power modulation amplitude on molten pool dynamics, spatter behavior, weld formation, and microstructure were systematically investigated. The results demonstrate that the tailored beam shaping strategy enhances molten pool stability, reduces spatter, and improves weld morphology. Quantitative analysis reveals that at a 40% power modulation amplitude, the maximum weld surface depression depth is reduced by 22.6%, and the HAZ width decreases by 23.9% compared to the unmodulated process, effectively mitigating coating cracking. Furthermore, the average grain size in the weld zone is refined by 22.5% (decreasing from 46.6 μm to 36.1 μm), while the proportion of high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) increases by 13.37%. This leads to a lower temperature gradient in the molten pool, a wider equiaxed grain zone, and refined grain structures. Moreover, the formation mechanism of equiaxed grains and grain refinement behavior under different modulation amplitudes were analyzed based on classical nucleation and growth theory. This work provides an effective approach for stabilizing the laser welding process and optimizing the microstructure and mechanical performance of Zn-Al-Mg coated steels.

Due to its excellent corrosion resistance, self-healing proper­ ties, and superior anti-rust performance, it has gradually replaced some stainless steels and aluminum alloys in various engineering applications, including photovoltaic brackets, rail transportation, and construction sectors [1,2]. The Zn-Al-Mg coating possesses a unique self-repairing corrosion protection property against cut-edge corrosion, attributed to the formation of a passivation film. This feature provides superior pro­ tection for both incisions and surface scratches. Unlike traditional galvanized coatings, when Zn-Al-Mg steel is sheared or scratched, a dense passivation film (composed of Zn-Al-Mg oxides and carbonates) forms on the exposed substrate. selected_writing_points.source_text: The welding of Zn-Al-Mg coated steel faces three major challenges. First, low-boiling-point elements such as zinc (Zn) and magnesium (Mg) evaporate intensely during laser welding, generating high-pressure metal vapor that violently disrupts the molten pool and keyhole stabil­ ity. This leads to welding defects such as spatter, undercutting, and porosity, severely compromising joint formation quality [5,6]. Second, zinc (Zn), aluminum (Al), and magnesium (Mg) can react with iron (Fe), carbon (C), and oxygen (O) in the molten pool to form various inter­ metallic compounds (e.g., Fe-Zn-Al phases, MgZn2), which are prone to microcrack initiation under external loads or residual welding stresses,

The effects of laser power modulation amplitude on molten pool dynamics, spatter behavior, weld formation, and microstructure were systematically investigated. The results demonstrate that the tailored beam shaping strategy enhances molten pool stability, reduces spatter, and improves weld morphology. Quantitative analysis reveals that at a 40% power modulation amplitude, the maximum weld surface depression depth is reduced by 22.6%, and the HAZ width decreases by 23.9% compared to the unmodulated process, effectively mitigating coating cracking. Furthermore, the average grain size in the weld zone is refined by 22.5% (decreasing from 46.6 μm to 36.1 μm), while the proportion of high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) increases by 13.37%. This leads to a lower temperature gradient in the molten pool, a wider equiaxed grain zone, and refined grain structures. Moreover, the formation mechanism of equiaxed grains and grain refinement behavior under different modulation amplitudes were analyzed based on classical nucleation and growth theory. This work provides an effective approach for stabilizing the laser welding process and optimizing the microstructure and mechanical performance of Zn-Al-Mg coated steels.

writing_points.source_text: Zinc-Aluminum-Magnesium (Zn-Al-Mg) coated steel is an emerging environmentally friendly material with excellent corrosion resistance and self-repairing corrosion protection property. However, the presence of the Zn- Al-Mg coating poses significant challenges during welding, including severe coating evaporation, complex metallurgical reactions, coating cracking in the heat-affected zone (HAZ), elemental segregation, and micro­ structural inhomogeneity. In this study, a novel spatio-temporal beam shaping strategy-combining full domain power modulation with galvanometer-scanning laser was proposed to achieve precise control of the molten pool through synergistic energy and mechanical coupling. Quantitative analysis reveals that at a 40% power modulation amplitude, the maximum weld surface depression depth is reduced by 22.6%, and the HAZ width decreases by 23.9% compared to the unmodulated process, effectively mitigating coating cracking. Furthermore, the average grain size in the weld zone is refined by 22.5% (decreasing from 46.6 μm to 36.1 μm), while the proportion of high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) increases by 13.37%. This leads to a lower temperature gradient in the molten pool, a wider equiaxed grain zone, and refined grain structures. Moreover, the formation mechanism of equiaxed grains and grain refinement behavior under different modulation amplitudes were analyzed based on classical nucleation and growth theory. This work provides an effective approach for stabilizing the laser welding process and optimizing the microstructure and mechanical performance of Zn-Al-Mg coated steels.
